channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Night Shift: Devil Division Brings 90s Anime OVA Grit to Draw Steel
item.content:encoded:
A new TTRPG crowdfunding campaign is turning some heads. Night Shift: Devil Division is the newest project by Slightly Reckless Games, and it combines the tactical gameplay of Draw Steel with the dark Shonen anime style.

The Night Shift: Devil Division Gamefound Campaign

According to its Backerkit campaign page, Night Shift: Devil Division is a TTRPG set in a dystopian version of 1990s Tokyo. This version of the sprawling metropolis is full of urban decay, economic squalor, and devils manifested from humanity's deepest fears. The game will comprise of two books: the Division core rulebook and the Devils bestiary.

The players are Operatives sent on behalf of the city to investigate Under Tokyo, an underground section of the city filled with societal misfits and devils. Your objectives are to detect, detain, or destroy devils that pose a threat. This may involve channeling your own skills and abilities or making pacts with those same devils for power.

On aesthetics along, Night Shift: Devil Division takes a lot from gritter late 80s/early 90s anime OVAs and more recent horror-adjacent shonen series. Bubblegum Crisis, Ghost in the Shell, Chainsaw Man, and Jujutsu Kaisen are all cited inspirations alongside videogames like Shin Megami Tensei, Devil May Cry.

As for the system powering this experience, it will be using a modified version of MCDM's Draw Steel, the tactical fantasy action TTRPG created by tabletop personality Matt Colville. It's a system that has continued to rise in popularity thanks to its high-quality core gameplay, ongoing of support via Crack The Sun, and a dedicated VTT known as The Codex.

There are notable gameplay differences between Night Shift: Devil Division and Draw Steel. Some of this includes streamlined resources like Momentum and Adrenaline, and recurring devil characters in the form of Buddies. There's also rules for Downtime, allowing for training arcs and world state changes, playful banter between combat rounds, and chase sequences.

The different pledge levels for Night Shift: Devil Division are as follows:

  • Digital Division for $37: PDF of the Division Core Rulebook
  • Digital Operative Bundle for $49: PDFs of Division, Devils, The Operative Starter Set, and Digital Starter Set (containing digital tokens, resource trackers, and handouts)
  • The Operative Starter Set for $56: Physical and digital version of The Operative Starter set
  • Division Hardcover for $64: Physical and PDF copies of the Division book and Digital Starter Set
  • Limited Edition Division Hardcover for $103: Campaign exclusive physical Limited Edition Division book, a PDF copy, and Digital Starter Set
  • Devil & Division Hardcover for $119: Physical and PDF copies of Division and Devils books and Digital Starter Set
  • Limited Edition Devils & Division Hardcover for $178: Physical Limited Edition and PDF copies of Division and Devils books, and Digital Starter Set
  • The Full Arsenal for $154: Physical and PDF copies of Division and Devils Books, the Operative Starter Set, and the Digital Starter Set
  • The Vault for $336: Everything in The Full Arsenal pledge level, physical Limited Edition Division and Devils book, a GM Screen, a Chase Sequence card deck, an A2 "On Leave" poster, a double-sided 1990s Tokyo map poster, a set of crystal dice, and a campaign exclusive slipcase

At the time of writing, Night Shift: Devil Division has raised £157,316, over five times its initial funding goal of £30,000. The campaign ends on Jun 4.

Ever since he was small, Tyler Chancey has had a deep, abiding love for video games and a tendency to think and overanalyze everything he enjoyed. This… More about Tyler

    Additional topics:

  • Crowdfunding

  • RPG



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/news/night-shift-devil-division-brings-90s-anime-ova-grit-to-gaming-tables
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Gilgamesh is Coming to Honkai: Star Rail, and He's Absolutely Chopped
item.content:encoded:
The latest update for Honkai: Star Rail, Patch 4.3, was just announced, and along with this patch comes the highly sought Mortenax Blade. While this is exciting for fans of the game, one topic has dominated the conversation: Gilgamesh.

Along with news on 4.3, Hoyoverse revealed two new characters for the Fate/Stay Night collaboration with Honkai: Star Rail. Originally, we had Saber and Archer, and joining them in Summer 2026 are Rin Tohsaka and Gilgamesh, both fan favorites.

So why, then, is Gilgamesh getting roasted so bad?

Gilgamesh is one of Fate/Stay Night's most iconic characters, but let's be honest, part of his appeal is his alluring looks. His majestic golden armor, slicked blonde hair, and concerningly dominant attitude capture the hearts and minds of viewers. 

So what's up with that outfit, then? 

It's designed by Type-Moon's very own Takeuchi, but it is, by all accounts, a fashion disaster. His chest is exposed but has long sleeves. The pants have these weird poofs at the end, and his shoes are armored and pointed. Eagle-eyed users on Twitter note this as a mishmash of Gilgamesh's various outfits all combined into one. 

In all honesty, it's playful cajoling. Consider his fashion sense in other games, namely his debut in Fate/Stay Night. His poofy white coat and black on black shirts and pants. Or his black tracksuit that he loves to roll with. He lived thousands of years ago -- cut him some slack. He either looks amazing, or he's a fashion disaster.

It is actually very exciting to see more characters from the Fate universe arrive in Honkai: Star Rail. Along with him comes Rin Tohsaka, who has a much better fashion sense. Rin Tohsaka will be on a banner for Honkai: Star Rail; meanwhile, HoYoverse says "During the collaboration period, players simply need to log in to the game for a chance to obtain the new character Gilgamesh or Archer for free." 

He will also be on a banner for additional copies of the character. Be sure to log in on July 24th and onward to partake in this event, or else you'll miss out on chopped Gilgamesh. 

Austin cut his teeth writing various  fan-fiction stories on the RuneScape forums when he was in elementary school. Later on, he developed a deep love… More about Austin



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/gilgamesh-is-coming-to-honkai-star-rail-and-hes-absolutely-chopped
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Star Fox Zero Is Actually Good, With This One Caveat
item.content:encoded:
Star Fox Zero was often regarded as the game that killed the longtime franchise. Well, at least until the recent announcement of Star Fox (2026). Regardless, Star Fox Zero is speculated to have sold poorly, similar to Star Fox Command on the DS, which was a handheld title. Zero, however, was a mainline entry on Nintendo's flagship console. Why did it fail when all the cards were in its favor? Moreover, why do people rail on it to this very day?

I wanted to find that out, and in order to do so, I called up my reliable pal Matt. He suggested playing Star Fox Zero in co-op. I was initially hesitant about this idea. Nintendo co-op titles tend to be half-baked in execution. Take, for example, Super Mario Galaxy, where one player controls Mario while another can shoot and collect star bits.

In other words, it's the younger sibling mode. Player 1 does all the work while you give Player 2 a menial task to keep them distracted. On the surface, Star Fox Zero has a similar premise. One player controls Fox's Arwing while the other handles shooting on the GamePad. It turns out, this might just be the optimal way to play the lowest point in the series. 

Star Fox Zero's a Co-Op Game, Through and Through

To understand why Star Fox Zero works and plays well, let's talk about how single player works. Only then can you really appreciate the burden co-op lifts. Other than being derivative, critics and consumers criticized Star Fox Zero for its convoluted controls. The GamePad is the culprit, and so is gyro aim, which is forced if you're on single player.

Gryo aim, my old nemesis. I'm a mouse-and-keyboard kind of guy, and in the absence of that, I settle for controllers. The last thing I'd want to do is use gyro aim to shoot. It's something I've never acclimated to or liked. So for Star Fox Zero to utilize gyro aim, well, that was almost a deal breaker.

In single player, you'd have to balance moving your Arwing, barrel-rolling, and avoiding obstacles while also using the GamePad to aim around. It was a muddled, overly complex control scheme that made Star Fox Zero way more difficult than it had to be.

In comes Matt, my co-pilot. While he focused solely on the gyro aim aspect—essentially acting as a secondary gunner—I could avoid obstacles and play Star Fox Zero more akin to Star Fox 64 instead (which happens to be one of my favorite games of all time).

Matt, who hadn't played co-op before, said this actually made Star Fox Zero fun, and I agree wholeheartedly. The GamePad has a cool feature where the screen is a cockpit view from the Arwing. Rather than looking up and down between screens, you get a focused, less janky experience by either, in Matt's case, viewing the game almost entirely from the cockpit, or, in my case, in a third-person view. 

A few missions in Star Fox Zero puts players in the cockpit of different vehicles, but it's the hovercraft-like Gyrowing that I found to be really neat with its co-op features. The player on the GamePad controls a little robot connected to a tether. This robot drops out from under the Gyrowing and can be used to hack objects in the environment.

Normally, this robot is inside the Gyrowing and the GamePad player shoots as they would in an Arwing or Landmaster. We found that you could dangle the robot in mid-air and it could still shoot, leading to some funny moments. Matt's view from the GamePad looked really fun to watch. 

Of course, this doesn't completely remove the jank from Star Fox Zero. Despite being a series staple, Star Fox Zero's 360 degree All-Range Mode can feel really difficult to control. I can only imagine how hard it is to contend with All-Range Mode's controls while also using the GamePad's gyro aim to land a shot.

With Star Fox (2026) releasing next month, my interest in the series was reignited, and I'm very glad that Matt proposed co-op. As a game where most of its ideas are derived from others in the series, the co-op mode feels like a fresh experience that you can't get from any of the others. Not only that, it removes the absolute jank of playing single player. Grab a friend and give it a shot, and you might just end up seeing Star Fox Zero is a more positive light.

Austin cut his teeth writing various  fan-fiction stories on the RuneScape forums when he was in elementary school. Later on, he developed a deep love… More about Austin



https://techraptor.net/gaming/opinions/star-fox-zero-is-actually-good-with-this-one-caveat
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
